Name: latency_optimized_kernel_fusion
Title: Accelerating Latency in GPU Inference through Adaptive Kernel Fusion
Short Hypothesis: This proposal posits that adaptive kernel fusion, informed by real-time profiling data, can significantly reduce latency in GPU inference tasks compared to existing static fusion strategies. This direction is necessary as existing methods often overlook the impact of dynamic workload characteristics on performance, leading to suboptimal configurations that fail to adapt to varying input distributions.
Related Work: Existing literature, such as 'Kernel Looping' which focuses on eliminating synchronization boundaries, highlights the necessity of optimizing GPU inference. However, it does not fully exploit real-time profiling data to adaptively adjust kernel fusion strategies based on the specific characteristics of input data. Our approach distinguishes itself by integrating adaptive profiling to derive optimized fusion configurations in real-time, which has not been explored in prior works.
Abstract: The demand for lower latency in GPU-accelerated inference is paramount as applications scale. Existing optimizations often rely on static kernel fusion methods that do not account for the dynamic nature of workloads. This proposal introduces an adaptive kernel fusion mechanism that utilizes real-time profiling data to optimize the fusion of CUDA kernels during inference. By analyzing occupancy, bandwidth utilization, and execution latency on-the-fly, our method intelligently combines kernels based on current workload characteristics, thereby minimizing synchronization overhead and maximizing throughput. We hypothesize that this adaptive approach will yield substantial improvements in latency and tokens-per-second metrics when benchmarked against static fusion strategies. The proposal includes a series of experiments designed to evaluate the performance improvements across various model architectures and input distributions. By focusing on empirical measurements and reproducible benchmarks, this research aims to contribute concrete systems advances in the field of efficient inference.
Experiments: 1. Baseline Comparison: Implement static kernel fusion strategies as baselines. Measure p50 and p99 latency, tokens-per-second, and energy per token for various model architectures (e.g., CNNs, Transformers). 2. Adaptive Kernel Fusion Implementation: Develop a profiling tool that gathers real-time kernel performance metrics. Use this data to inform fusion decisions dynamically. 3. Performance Evaluation: Conduct A/B testing between static and adaptive kernel fusion on multiple GPUs (NVIDIA V100, A100). Measure and compare latency, throughput, and utilization metrics. 4. Profiling Analysis: Collect detailed profiling data (occupancy, bandwidth, stalls) to attribute performance gains to specific hardware optimizations during adaptive fusion.
Risk Factors and Limitations: - Overhead of profiling: Real-time profiling may introduce overhead that could negate some performance gains. This must be carefully managed and evaluated. - Complexity in implementation: Developing a system that can dynamically adapt kernel fusion may increase the complexity of the implementation. - Hardware dependency: Results may vary across different hardware configurations, limiting generalizability. Controlled experiments will be necessary to quantify these effects.
